13: Asa’s deposition of his mother Maacah from the position of gᵉḇîrāh — wayyasirāh ʾet maʿăḵāh ʾimmô mig gᵉḇîrāh — involves the most politically significant female office in the Judahite monarchy. The gᵉḇîrāh (literally ‘great lady,’ from gāḇar, to be strong/great) was a formal court position, not merely a familial designation: the queen mother had official standing, was present at accession ceremonies, and could intercede with the king as a recognized court actor (1 Kings 2:19-20: Bathsheba as gᵉḇîrāh approaches Solomon, who rises, bows, and seats her at his right hand with a throne of her own). The Ugaritic texts document a comparable institution (rbtn, ‘great lady’) at the Ugaritic court, and Hittite records show a formal ‘Great Queen’ (MUNUS.LUGAL-uš) position. The gᵉḇîrāh was particularly significant in Judah because the king had multiple wives but only one queen mother — making her the most stable senior female figure across reigns. Maacah’s deposition is simultaneously a formal political act and a religious one: she is removed because she made ‘a horrifying thing (mipleset) for Asherah,’ and Asa burns the object in the Kidron. The Kuntillet Ajrud inscriptions (ca. 800 BCE) mentioning ‘YHWH and his Asherah’ document the popular syncretism that allowed an Asherah object in the court of YHWH’s appointed king’s mother — the precise situation Asa’s reform addresses.
1: The regnal formula for Abijam (= Abijah in Chronicles) synchronizes his accession: 'In the eighteenth year of King Jeroboam son of Nebat, Abijam began to reign over Judah.' The synchronistic regnal formula — giving the accession year of the southern king relative to the reigning northern king — is characteristic of the Deuteronomistic History's chronological framework. The system depends on two independent year-counting traditions (Judahite and Israelite) that differ in when the new year begins and how accession years are counted, explaining apparent discrepancies in modern calculations of biblical chronology.
2: Abijam's regnal summary: three years in Jerusalem. His mother is identified as Maacah daughter of Abishalom — the same Maacah who will appear in v13 as queen mother (gᵉḇîrāh). The identification of the king's mother is standard in Kings' evaluative formulas; the queen mother was a significant political office in Judah, with a formal title and institutional role (see on v13). Abishalom is likely a variant spelling of Absalom (cf. 2 Chronicles 11:20-21), making Maacah David's great-granddaughter through Absalom.
3: The evaluation: Abijam walked in all the sins of his father. The Deuteronomistic evaluative formula uses the verb hālak (walk) to describe covenant faithfulness or apostasy — walking in YHWH's ways or in the ways of apostate predecessors. Abijam's heart (lēḇāḇ) was not wholly devoted to YHWH his God — the wholeness formula (šālēm, complete, undivided) is the standard of the Deuteronomistic evaluation; David's divided not-wholeness remains the paradox that the narrator resolves by invoking the Davidic covenant.
4: The exception clause: 'Yet for David's sake the LORD his God gave him a lamp in Jerusalem, raising up his son after him and establishing Jerusalem.' The nîr (lamp/lamp-wick) for David — preserving the Davidic line despite Abijam's failure — is the covenant-preservation motif that runs through Kings. The 'lamp' metaphor appears also in 2 Samuel 21:17 ('you shall not go out with us to battle, lest you quench the lamp of Israel'); the continuity of the Davidic house is the lamp that YHWH tends despite individual kings' failures.
5: The Davidic exception: David did what was right in YHWH's eyes and did not turn aside from anything he commanded him all the days of his life, except in the matter of Uriah the Hittite. The parenthetical exception — the Bathsheba-Uriah affair of 2 Sam 11-12 — is the one recorded blot on David's record, acknowledged rather than suppressed. This honest narrative accounting contrasts with ancient royal annals that typically omit royal failures. The exception clause's presence underscores that the evaluation of David is historical, not hagiographic.
6: War between Rehoboam and Jeroboam continued all the days of Abijam's life — or, in some manuscripts, 'all the days of his life [Abijam's].' The textual note reflects a variation between MT and some manuscripts. The persistent state of border conflict between Judah and Israel — rather than decisive battle — characterizes the early divided monarchy period. The border zone between the two kingdoms in Benjamin (cf. 1 Kings 15 vv17-22) was the site of intermittent military pressure rather than open war.
7: The source citation: 'Are not the acts of Abijam... written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Judah?' The sēper diḇrê hayyāmîm lᵉmalkê yᵉhûḏāh (Book of the Words/Chronicles of the Days of the Kings of Judah) is one of the Deuteronomistic Historian's principal sources, cited 15 times for Judahite kings. This is a distinct document from the canonical 1–2 Chronicles; it was a court record or official annals that the historian had access to but which has not survived independently.
8: Abijam died and was buried in the city of David — the traditional Davidic tomb-complex on the southeastern ridge of Jerusalem. The burial formula ('slept with his fathers' + burial location) is the standard closing formula for Judahite kings who died in office. The David tomb-city as the burial site for all Judahite kings before Hezekiah signals the continuity of the Davidic dynasty and its territorial identification with the original city of David.
9: Asa's accession synchronization: 'In the twentieth year of Jeroboam king of Israel, Asa began to reign over Judah.' Asa's reign of 41 years (v10) makes him one of the longer-reigning Judahite kings. His reign overlaps substantially with those of Baasha (1 Kings 16:6-7) and the beginning of the Omride dynasty in the north. The 41-year reign is remarkable: Asa would have been a child at accession (his mother Maacah is still the queen mother from Abijam's reign, suggesting Asa came to the throne young).
10: Asa's mother is also named Maacah daughter of Abishalom — the same person as Abijam's mother, meaning Asa's mother and grandmother are the same woman, or (more likely) that Maacah is Asa's grandmother who continues as gᵉḇîrāh. The retention of the queen mother position across two reigns is attested in other ancient Near Eastern courts; the office did not automatically transfer to the new king's biological mother but could be held by a senior woman of the royal household.
11: The positive Deuteronomistic evaluation: 'Asa did what was right in the eyes of YHWH as David his father had done.' Only a handful of Judahite kings receive this unqualified positive assessment: Asa, Jehoshaphat, Hezekiah, and Josiah. The comparison to David remains the benchmark. The Hebrew kᵉdāwid ʾāḇîhû (as David his father) treats David as the paradigm king against whom all successors are measured — the standard established at 2 Sam 7 is the measuring rod of Kings.
12: Asa's reform program: he removed the male cult prostitutes (qᵉḏēšîm) from the land and removed all the idols (gillûlîm) that his fathers had made. The qᵉḏēšîm (singular qāḏēš) are sacred functionaries associated with Canaanite religious practice; the exact nature of their role is debated, with 'cult prostitute' a traditional translation and 'sacred functionary' or 'devotee' as alternatives. Their presence alongside gillûlîm (a contemptuous term for idols, from gillāh, dung-pellet) identifies the Canaanite religious infrastructure that has penetrated Judah since Rehoboam.
14: The qualifying note: the high places were not removed, though Asa's heart was wholly true to YHWH all his days. The tension between the Deuteronomistic ideal (all worship centralized in Jerusalem) and the persistent reality of local high places (bāmôt) recurs throughout Kings. Even reforming kings who receive positive evaluations fail to abolish the high places. Only Hezekiah (2 Kings 18:4) and Josiah (2 Kings 23:8) are credited with removing them. The heart-wholeness formula here contrasts with Abijam's divided heart (v3).
15: Asa brought into the house of YHWH the sacred gifts his father had dedicated and his own sacred gifts — silver, gold, and vessels. The practice of dedicating captured or tributary precious metals to the temple treasury is attested throughout the ancient Near East; temple treasuries functioned as both sacred deposits and state reserves. Asa's act of bringing his father's dedicatory gifts reverses the pattern of temple-plundering (as under Shishak, 1 Kings 14:26) — filling rather than emptying the treasury.
16: War between Asa and Baasha of Israel all their days. Baasha's reign (1 Kings 15:33) begins in Asa's third year; the border conflict between them centers on the Benjamin territory between the two kingdoms. The political geography of the divided monarchy placed the border approximately at Mizpah and Ramah — towns in Benjamin that Benjamin's traditional territory shared between the two kingdoms, making this zone perpetually contested.
17: Baasha built Ramah — possibly Tell er-Ram, about 8 km north of Jerusalem — to prevent anyone from going out or coming in to Asa king of Judah. Ramah's fortification as a border fort controlling the main north-south road through Benjamin was a strategic move to block communications between Judah and the northern territories. The tactic of controlling road-towns to restrict movement is a classic ANE military strategy; the Assyrians used similar fortified road-posts to control subject territories.
18: Asa took all the silver and gold remaining in the treasuries of the house of YHWH and the house of the king and sent them to Ben-hadad son of Tabrimmon son of Hezion king of Aram-Damascus. The three-generation genealogy of Ben-hadad establishes this as Ben-hadad I, the founder of the Aramean dynasty at Damascus. The name Ben-hadad (bar-hadad, son of Hadad) is a dynastic throne-name; the historical Ben-hadad I is attested in the Melqart Stele (found 1940 near Aleppo), which mentions his father Tabrimmon. The temple treasury as state financial reserve — available for tributary payments — is consistent with ancient Near Eastern practice.
19: The treaty proposal: 'There is a covenant between me and you, between my father and your father' — invoking a previous treaty relationship. The bᵉrît (covenant/treaty) between Judah and Aram was a diplomatic alliance needing renewal. Asa's request that Ben-hadad break his treaty (bᵉrît) with Baasha and align with Judah instead is a classic piece of ancient Near Eastern diplomacy: buying an alliance by invoking prior treaty bonds and offering fresh tribute.
20: Ben-hadad sends his armies against the northern cities: Ijon, Dan, Abel-maim, and all Naphtali. The list of northern territories targeted by the Aramean invasion matches the later Assyrian list of northern territories captured — Dan and Abel-maim (Abel-beth-maacah) appear in both. The Aramaean campaign effectively opened a northern front, forcing Baasha to abandon his fortification project at Ramah and withdraw to Tirzah (his capital, v21).
21: Baasha abandoned Ramah and lived in Tirzah. Tirzah (ṯirsāh) — the northern capital identified with Tell el-Farah (North), excavated by Roland de Vaux — was the seat of the northern monarchy from Jeroboam through Omri. Its name means 'pleasing, beautiful' (Song of Solomon 6:4 uses it as a byword for beauty). The abandonment of the Ramah fortification allowed Asa to exploit the situation immediately.
22: King Asa proclaimed a levy on all Judah — no one exempt — to carry away the stones and timber of Ramah. The mas (forced labor levy) was typically organized by the royal administration for public building projects (Solomon used it for the temple and his palace complex). Asa's rapid reuse of Baasha's building materials at Geba and Mizpah shows both the urgency of the response and the pragmatic reuse of worked stone — common in the ancient Near East, where quarrying and dressing stone was labor-intensive.
23: Asa's reign summary mentions his building projects and his power, but closes with a note about his foot disease in his old age. The Chronicler adds detail (2 Chronicles 16:12) that Asa sought physicians rather than YHWH for his disease — a theological evaluation of his final years. The foot disease (regel, lit. 'foot/leg') is unspecified medically; the phrase 'his disease was severe' (kî ḥālāh lᵉḥŏlî) indicates its seriousness.
24: Asa died and was buried in the city of David. The burial formula marks the end of his 41-year reign. The Chronicler's parallel (2 Chronicles 16:14) adds that they made a very great fire in his honor, laid him on a bier filled with spices and various blended preparations, and burned incense for him — suggesting a royal funeral with spice-burning honors, attested in Neo-Babylonian funeral texts as a mark of royal honor for the deceased.
25: Nadab son of Jeroboam accession formula: 'In the second year of Asa king of Judah, Nadab son of Jeroboam began to reign over Israel and reigned two years.' The synchronization with Asa's second year places Nadab's brief reign very early in Asa's long reign. Nadab is the only son of Jeroboam to reign; the Ahijah prophecy (1 Kings 14:10) predicted the destruction of Jeroboam's house, and Nadab's reign and death accomplish it.
26: Nadab's negative evaluation: he did evil in YHWH's eyes and walked in the way of his father and in the sin by which he made Israel sin. The ḥaṭṭāʾt yārāḇʿām (sin of Jeroboam) formula — first applied here to a successor king — will become the standard evaluative phrase for all northern kings. The formula references Jeroboam's golden calves at Bethel and Dan (1 Kings 12:28-30) as the definitional apostasy of the northern kingdom.
27: Baasha son of Ahijah of the house of Issachar conspired against Nadab and struck him at Gibbethon. Gibbethon (gibbᵉṯôn) — a Danite city in the Shephelah controlled by the Philistines at this period (cf. 1 Kings 16:15-17 where Gibbethon siege recurs) — was apparently the site of an Israelite siege operation where the army was concentrated, providing the opportunity for Baasha's military coup. The pattern of military commanders using sieges as the occasion for coups recurs in Israelite history.
28: Baasha killed Nadab in the third year of Asa king of Judah and reigned in his place. The brief, stark formula for regicide — 'and he struck him and killed him' — becomes a pattern in the north (cf. Zimri, Shallum, Pekah). The northern monarchy's political instability contrasts with the unbroken Davidic succession in the south: in the same period Judah has one dynasty, the north cycles through multiple dynasties.
29: As soon as Baasha was king he struck down all the house of Jeroboam. The dynastic elimination — fulfilling Ahijah's oracle (1 Kings 14:10) — is the standard ancient Near Eastern practice for a new dynasty consolidating power: eliminating rival claimants to prevent future challenges. The phrase 'he did not leave to Jeroboam any who breathed' echoes the ḥērem language of holy war applied to a royal house.
30: The theological interpretation: because of the sins of Jeroboam which he sinned and which he made Israel sin, and because of the anger with which he provoked YHWH the God of Israel to anger. The Deuteronomistic pattern of connecting dynastic punishment to prior sin: Jeroboam's sin produces the oracle; the oracle produces the judgment; the judgment is accomplished through a human agent (Baasha) who is himself then judged for the violence (v29: 'according to the word of YHWH which he spoke by his servant Ahijah').
31: The source formula: 'Are not the acts of Nadab... written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel?' The parallel source for the northern kingdom — the sēper diḇrê hayyāmîm lᵉmalkê yiśrāʾēl (Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel) — is cited 17 times for northern kings. Like its Judahite counterpart, this was an official court record not otherwise surviving but used by the Deuteronomistic Historian.
32: War between Asa and Baasha king of Israel all their days. The formula links the northern and southern narratives: the same border conflict described from Judah's perspective (vv16-22) is noted here at the close of Nadab's entry as the ongoing political reality. The Baasha narrative proper begins at v33; this verse provides the political backdrop for understanding why the Ben-hadad alliance (v18-20) was so significant.
33: Baasha's accession formula: 'In the third year of Asa king of Judah, Baasha son of Ahijah began to reign over all Israel in Tirzah, and reigned twenty-four years.' Tirzah as capital: Baasha inherits and maintains the previous northern capital. His 24-year reign gives the north political stability under his dynasty (supplemented by his son Elah's 2-year reign), though the Deuteronomistic evaluation (v34) groups him with Jeroboam's sin. Baasha has the longest reign of any northern king between Jeroboam I and Ahab.
34: Baasha's negative evaluation using the full ḥaṭṭāʾt yārāḇʿām formula. The irony is explicit: Baasha was YHWH's instrument to destroy Jeroboam's house (v29: 'according to the word of YHWH'), yet Baasha himself walks in the sin of Jeroboam. The judgment oracle against Baasha (1 Kings 16:1-4) uses identical language to the oracle against Jeroboam (1 Kings 14:10-11) — dynastic repetition showing the pattern of sin, oracle, judgment recurring throughout the northern monarchy.
